_disclaimer: ALL ITEMS IN THIS FILE ARE SYNTHETIC PLACEHOLDERS, hand-authored to exercise the LSAT Item contract. They are NOT real LSAT items and must NOT be used as study material. Real items must be sourced from official LSAC PrepTests with redistribution licensing (D-SR11). Every item carries SyntheticFlag=SYNTHETIC.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Every member of the debate team is a senior. Priya is not a senior. So Priya is not a member of the debate team.
items.Stem: Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its reasoning to the argument above?
items.ChoiceA: Every published novel has an ISBN. This manuscript has no ISBN. So it is not a published novel.
items.ChoiceB: All cats are mammals. Rex is a mammal. So Rex is a cat.
items.ChoiceC: Most athletes train daily. Sam trains daily. So Sam is an athlete.
items.ChoiceD: No fish are mammals. A whale is a mammal. So a whale is not a fish.
items.ChoiceE: All roses are flowers. This is a flower. So it may be a rose.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. Same valid form: all X are Y; this is not Y; so this is not X (modus tollens).
items.WhyWrongB: This affirms the consequent (mammal, therefore cat) — an invalid, different form.
items.WhyWrongC: Uses 'most' and affirms a property — invalid and different.
items.WhyWrongD: Valid, but built on a negative universal premise — a different form.
items.WhyWrongE: Draws only a 'may' conclusion from a converse — different structure.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] If the bakery raises prices, some regulars will leave. If some regulars leave, weekly revenue will fall. So if the bakery raises prices, weekly revenue will fall.
items.Stem: The reasoning in which one of the following is most parallel to the reasoning above?
items.ChoiceA: Either the door is locked or it is open. It is not locked. So it is open.
items.ChoiceB: If you study, you pass. You did not study. So you did not pass.
items.ChoiceC: The store is open only if the lights are on. The lights are on. So the store is open.
items.ChoiceD: If it rains, the game is canceled. The game was canceled. So it rained.
items.ChoiceE: If the alarm rings, students exit. If students exit, the hall empties. So if the alarm rings, the hall empties.
items.WhyWrongA: Disjunctive syllogism — a different valid form.
items.WhyWrongB: Denies the antecedent — invalid, different form.
items.WhyWrongC: Treats a necessary condition as sufficient — invalid.
items.WhyWrongD: Affirms the consequent — invalid, different form.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. Same valid chained conditionals (hypothetical syllogism).

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] This restaurant must be excellent, because it is always crowded, and crowded restaurants are the good ones.
items.Stem: Which one of the following contains reasoning most similar to the flawed reasoning above?
items.ChoiceA: She must be tired, since she yawned, and tired people yawn.
items.ChoiceB: This book must be worth reading, since it is a bestseller, and bestsellers are the books worth reading.
items.ChoiceC: The plan will succeed, because the team worked very hard on it.
items.ChoiceD: This medicine works, because a doctor prescribed it, and doctors understand medicine.
items.ChoiceE: The bridge is safe, because engineers inspected it thoroughly last week.
items.WhyWrongA: This affirms the consequent (yawned, so tired) — a different flaw.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Same 'popularity equals quality' equation used as a premise.
items.WhyWrongC: This appeals to effort, not to popularity.
items.WhyWrongD: This is an appeal to authority — a different flaw.
items.WhyWrongE: This is not obviously flawed in the same way.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Every novel in the display is a hardcover. Every hardcover in the display is discounted. So every novel in the display is discounted.
items.Stem: Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its reasoning to the argument above?
items.ChoiceA: Some birds can swim. Some swimmers are fast. So some birds are fast.
items.ChoiceB: Every oak is a tree. This plant is not an oak. So this plant is not a tree.
items.ChoiceC: Every senator is a citizen. Maria is a citizen. So Maria is a senator.
items.ChoiceD: Every plant in the greenhouse is a fern. Every fern in the greenhouse needs shade. So every plant in the greenhouse needs shade.
items.ChoiceE: No reptile is a bird. Every sparrow is a bird. So no sparrow is a reptile.
items.WhyWrongA: Chains two 'some' premises — invalid and structurally different.
items.WhyWrongB: Denies the antecedent of a universal — invalid, different form.
items.WhyWrongC: Affirms a shared property to identify the subject — invalid, different form.
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. Same valid chain of universals: all X are Y; all Y are Z; so all X are Z.
items.WhyWrongE: Valid, but built from a negative universal premise — a different form.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] If the museum received the grant, then it expanded its east wing. The museum did not expand its east wing. So the museum did not receive the grant.
items.Stem: The reasoning in which one of the following is most parallel to the reasoning above?
items.ChoiceA: If the road is icy, then the bus is late. The bus is late. So the road is icy.
items.ChoiceB: If the recipe contains yeast, then the dough rises. The dough did not rise. So the recipe did not contain yeast.
items.ChoiceC: If the plant is watered, then it grows. The plant was not watered. So it did not grow.
items.ChoiceD: If a student is enrolled, then she has an ID card. Tom has an ID card. So Tom is enrolled.
items.ChoiceE: If the store is open, then the sign is lit. The store is open. So the sign is lit.
items.WhyWrongA: Affirms the consequent — invalid, different form.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Same valid modus tollens: if P then Q; not Q; so not P.
items.WhyWrongC: Denies the antecedent — invalid, different form.
items.WhyWrongD: Affirms the consequent — invalid, different form.
items.WhyWrongE: Valid modus ponens — a different valid form.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Nothing is better than lifelong happiness. A modest pension is better than nothing. So a modest pension is better than lifelong happiness.
items.Stem: Which one of the following contains reasoning most similar to the flawed reasoning above?
items.ChoiceA: Every event has a cause. This event occurred. So this event had a cause.
items.ChoiceB: No one should break a promise. Some promises are unwise. So no one should question unwise promises.
items.ChoiceC: All that glitters is not gold. This coin glitters. So this coin is not gold.
items.ChoiceD: Time heals all wounds. A bruise is a kind of wound. So time heals all bruises.
items.ChoiceE: Nothing is more valuable than good health. A short walk is more valuable than nothing. So a short walk is more valuable than good health.
items.WhyWrongA: This is a valid application, not the equivocation flaw.
items.WhyWrongB: Shifts scope from breaking to questioning — a different error.
items.WhyWrongC: Turns on the scope of negation, a different ambiguity.
items.WhyWrongD: A valid instantiation of a universal — not the same flaw.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. Same equivocation: 'nothing' as a universal comparison, then as an object of comparison.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The three students I happened to ask all prefer online classes. So most students at the college prefer online classes.
items.Stem: Which one of the following contains reasoning most similar to the flawed reasoning above?
items.ChoiceA: The first two apples I tasted from the crate were sweet. So every apple in the crate is sweet.
items.ChoiceB: Every apple I have ever eaten has been sweet. So this apple is probably sweet too.
items.ChoiceC: My car has broken down twice this month. So my car is unreliable.
items.ChoiceD: Leading economists agree the policy will work. So the policy will work.
items.ChoiceE: A survey of five thousand voters shows a clear lead. So the candidate is ahead in the race.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. Same flaw: a broad conclusion drawn from a tiny, unrepresentative sample.
items.WhyWrongB: Draws on a large body of experience — a reasonable induction, not the same flaw.
items.WhyWrongC: Concludes about the same single object, not a generalization to a population.
items.WhyWrongD: Relies on authority rather than a small sample — a different move.
items.WhyWrongE: Rests on a large sample — structurally different.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Only registered members may enter the lounge. No one under 18 is a registered member. So no one under 18 may enter the lounge.
items.Stem: Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its reasoning to the argument above?
items.ChoiceA: Only ticket holders may board the train. No child traveling without a guardian is a ticket holder. So no child traveling without a guardian may board the train.
items.ChoiceB: Only registered citizens may vote in the election. Ravi is a registered citizen. So Ravi may vote.
items.ChoiceC: Club members receive the monthly newsletter. Lin received the monthly newsletter. So Lin is a club member.
items.ChoiceD: Only the store manager can approve refunds. This refund was approved. So the store manager approved it.
items.ChoiceE: All club members pay annual dues. Some who pay dues are new arrivals. So some new arrivals are members.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. Same valid form: X requires Y; group lacks Y; so group cannot X.
items.WhyWrongB: Treats a necessary condition (citizenship) as sufficient — invalid, different form.
items.WhyWrongC: Affirms the consequent — invalid, different form.
items.WhyWrongD: Valid but reasons about a single approved case, not a whole group.
items.WhyWrongE: Chains 'all' and 'some' to an existential conclusion — different structure.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The missing report is either in the archive or on the server. It is not in the archive. So it is on the server.
items.Stem: The reasoning in which one of the following is most parallel to the reasoning above?
items.ChoiceA: The suspect was either at home or at work. He was at work. So he was not at home.
items.ChoiceB: The keys are either in the drawer or in the car. They are not in the drawer. So they are in the car.
items.ChoiceC: Tomorrow it will either rain or snow. It will rain. So it will not snow.
items.ChoiceD: If the file is corrupted, it will not open. The file will not open. So the file is corrupted.
items.ChoiceE: If the light is red, you must stop. The light is red. So you must stop.
items.WhyWrongA: Affirms one disjunct to deny the other — invalid, different form.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Same valid disjunctive syllogism: A or B; not A; so B.
items.WhyWrongC: Affirms one disjunct to deny the other — invalid for an inclusive 'or.'
items.WhyWrongD: Affirms the consequent — invalid, different form.
items.WhyWrongE: Valid modus ponens — a different valid form.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The oak is taller than the maple. The maple is taller than the birch. So the oak is taller than the birch.
items.Stem: Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its reasoning to the argument above?
items.ChoiceA: Ana scored higher than Ben. Ben scored higher than Cara. So Ana is the smartest person in the class.
items.ChoiceB: The train is faster than the bus. The bus is cheaper than the train. So the train is faster than it is cheap.
items.ChoiceC: The novel is longer than the novella. The novella is longer than the short story. So the novel is longer than the short story.
items.ChoiceD: The red box is heavier than the blue box. The blue box is heavier than the green box. So the green box is the lightest of the three.
items.ChoiceE: Sara is older than Tom. Tom is older than Lee. So Lee is the youngest of everyone.
items.WhyWrongA: Leaps from a ranking to an unsupported superlative about intelligence.
items.WhyWrongB: Mixes two different relations (speed and price) — incoherent, different structure.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. Same valid transitivity of a single 'greater than' relation across three items.
items.WhyWrongD: Draws a superlative conclusion rather than the simple transitive one.
items.WhyWrongE: Overreaches to 'the youngest of everyone' beyond the three items compared.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] A student can graduate only if she completes the thesis. Maria graduated. So Maria completed the thesis.
items.Stem: The reasoning in which one of the following is most parallel to the reasoning above?
items.ChoiceA: The alarm sounds whenever there is smoke. There is smoke now. So the alarm will sound.
items.ChoiceB: A runner wins only if she trains. This runner did not train. So this runner did not win.
items.ChoiceC: A plant blooms only if it is watered. This plant was watered. So this plant bloomed.
items.ChoiceD: A car passes inspection only if its brakes work. This car passed inspection. So this car's brakes work.
items.ChoiceE: You may enter the exhibit only if you have a ticket. You have a ticket. So you may enter the exhibit.
items.WhyWrongA: Valid modus ponens from a 'whenever' premise — a different form.
items.WhyWrongB: Valid modus tollens — a different valid form.
items.WhyWrongC: Treats watering (necessary) as sufficient for blooming — invalid, different form.
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. Same valid form: X requires Y; X occurred; so Y occurred.
items.WhyWrongE: Treats the necessary condition (a ticket) as sufficient for entry — invalid.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] No alloy that rusts is suitable for the ship's hull. Every steel that lacks chromium rusts. This component is made of steel that lacks chromium. So this component is not suitable for the ship's hull.
items.Stem: Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its reasoning to the argument above?
items.ChoiceA: No reptile is warm-blooded. This particular animal is warm-blooded. So this animal is certainly not a reptile at all.
items.ChoiceB: Every soft metal scratches easily. Gold is well known to be a soft metal. So gold, too, scratches easily.
items.ChoiceC: No bridge that goes uninspected is safe to cross. This bridge is safe to cross. So this bridge must have been inspected.
items.ChoiceD: All fully ripe peaches are sweet to the taste. This peach is not at all sweet. So this peach cannot be ripe.
items.ChoiceE: No plant that needs full sun thrives in shade. Every fern of this variety needs full sun. This plant is a fern of that variety. So this plant will not thrive in shade.
items.WhyWrongA: This is a single-step deduction; it lacks the middle universal that the original chains through.
items.WhyWrongB: This is a valid step to a positive conclusion — a different form from the original.
items.WhyWrongC: This reasons from an effect back to a condition and ends affirmatively — not the original's negative chain.
items.WhyWrongD: This is a direct one-step deduction with no intermediate category — a shorter, different structure.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. It matches the three-step chain: nothing with the property qualifies; all of a class have the property; this is in the class; so it does not qualify.
